questions.question: Calculate the Real GDP for Year 2 using Year 1 as the base year, if Year 2 Nominal GDP is \$600 billion and the price level has increased by 20% since Year 1.
questions.explanation: A 20% price increase means the GDP Deflator = 120. Real GDP = Nominal GDP ÷ (120 ÷ 100) = \$600B ÷ 1.20 = \$500 billion.

questions.question: Which of the following occurs to the value of Real GDP if an economy's production of goods increases by 5% while all prices simultaneously decrease by 5%?
questions.explanation: Real GDP measures output at constant base year prices. A 5% increase in production raises Real GDP by 5%. Price changes affect only Nominal GDP, not Real GDP.

questions.question: How does an increase in the expected rate of inflation influence the Phillips curves?
options: The short-run Phillips curve shifts downward
The short-run Phillips curve shifts upward
The long-run Phillips curve shifts rightward
The long-run Phillips curve shifts leftward
questions.explanation: An increase in expected inflation shifts the short-run Phillips curve upward (rightward)—at every unemployment rate, actual inflation is now higher. The long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate and does not shift with changes in expected inflation.

questions.question: If the GDP Deflator in 2025 was 110 and in 2026 it is 115, what is the annual inflation rate as measured by this index?
Which type of unemployment is characterized by workers who are laid off due to a seasonal decline in the demand for tourism and hospitality services?
questions.explanation: Seasonal unemployment results from predictable changes in demand tied to seasons or weather. Tourism, hospitality, and agriculture are typical examples.

questions.question: Identify the type of unemployment that remains even when the economy is operating at its potential level of output.
questions.explanation: At potential GDP, cyclical unemployment is zero. The natural rate of unemployment—frictional plus structural—persists even at full employment.

questions.question: In a labor force of 120,000 where 108,000 are employed, calculate the official unemployment rate.
How is the unemployment rate affected when 'underemployed' workers who want full-time jobs are only able to find part-time work?
options: It rises because they are partially unemployed
It falls because they are now working
It remains unchanged because they are counted as employed
questions.explanation: Underemployed part-time workers are classified as employed. The official unemployment rate does not reflect their situation, understating true labor market slack.

questions.question: The crowding-out effect is the tendency for a government budget deficit to raise the _____ and _____ investment.
questions.explanation: A government deficit increases the demand for loanable funds, pushing up the real interest rate. Higher rates make private investment more expensive, reducing it—this is the crowding-out effect.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the impact on the Aggregate Demand (AD) curve if the domestic currency significantly depreciates against all major foreign currencies?
options: AD shifts left as imports become cheaper
AD shifts right as net exports increase
AD shifts left as exports become more expensive
questions.explanation: Depreciation makes exports cheaper for foreigners and imports more expensive domestically. Net exports (X – M) increase, shifting AD rightward.

questions.question: Which of the following factors would cause a simultaneous rightward shift in both the Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS) and Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS) curves?
options: A temporary decrease in energy prices
An increase in the expected future price level
questions.explanation: Technological progress permanently expands the economy's productive capacity. Both SRAS and LRAS shift rightward, reflecting higher output potential.

questions.question: In the AD-AS model, what is the result of a decrease in consumer confidence on the equilibrium price level and Real GDP in the short run?
options: Both price level and Real GDP increase
Both price level and Real GDP decrease
Price level rises and Real GDP falls
Price level falls and Real GDP rises
questions.explanation: Lower consumer confidence reduces consumption, shifting AD leftward. The new short-run equilibrium has a lower price level and lower Real GDP.

questions.question: Which curve shifts to the left during the self-correction process if the economy is currently operating in an inflationary gap with rising nominal wages?
questions.explanation: Rising nominal wages increase firms' costs, shifting SRAS leftward. This returns output to potential at a higher price level, closing the inflationary gap.

questions.question: The federal government debt ______ when the federal government runs a deficit and ______ when the federal government runs a surplus.
questions.explanation: Deficits add to the national debt as the government borrows to cover the shortfall. Surpluses reduce the debt as the government repays past borrowing.

questions.question: What is the expected effect on the price level and unemployment when a government uses expansionary policy to close a recessionary gap?
options: Both price level and unemployment fall
Both price level and unemployment rise
questions.explanation: Expansionary policy (fiscal or monetary) shifts AD rightward. Output and the price level rise, and unemployment falls back toward the natural rate.

questions.question: An income tax cut that improves the incentive to work will have which of the following outcomes?
options: Potential GDP rises and after-tax real wages rise
Potential GDP rises but after-tax real wages stay flat
Potential GDP is flat but real wages rise
Employment rises but real wages stay flat
questions.explanation: A tax cut that improves work incentives increases labor supply, expanding potential GDP. Workers also keep more of each dollar earned, so the after-tax real wage rises.

questions.question: What is the specific term for the period of the business cycle that occurs between the trough and the peak?
questions.explanation: The expansion (recovery) phase runs from the trough (lowest point) to the peak (highest point). It features rising Real GDP and falling unemployment.

questions.question: If an increase in disposable income of \$2,000 results in an increase in consumption of \$1,500, what is the Marginal Propensity to Save (MPS)?
questions.explanation: Change in saving = \$2,000 – \$1,500 = \$500. MPS = \$500 ÷ \$2,000 = 0.25. So 25% of additional income is saved.

questions.question: Calculate the Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) if the government spending multiplier is exactly 5.
questions.explanation: Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 – MPC) = 5 → 1 – MPC = 0.2 → MPC = 0.8.

questions.question: Which of the following represents the intercept of the consumption function on a graph where consumption is on the vertical axis and disposable income is on the horizontal axis?
questions.explanation: The vertical intercept is autonomous consumption—the level of consumption when disposable income is zero, financed by savings, borrowing, or wealth.

questions.question: Given the consumption function C = 100 + 0.8Y and a Real GDP of \$1,000, calculate the total level of aggregate saving.
What is the total change in Real GDP if the government increases spending by \$50 billion and the MPS is 0.25?
questions.explanation: Multiplier = 1 ÷ MPS = 1 ÷ 0.25 = 4. ΔGDP = 4 × \$50B = \$200 billion.

questions.question: If the MPC is 0.9, calculate the tax multiplier and determine the direction of the change in Real GDP following a tax increase.
questions.explanation: Tax multiplier = –MPC ÷ (1 – MPC) = –0.9 ÷ 0.1 = –9. A tax increase reduces disposable income, so Real GDP decreases.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal actions is specifically intended to shift the Aggregate Demand curve to the right?
options: Reducing government purchases of public goods
Increasing government spending on public works
questions.explanation: Expansionary fiscal policy shifts AD right. Increased government spending directly adds to AD, while tax cuts boost disposable income. Both stimulate the economy.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the impact of a decrease in government spending on the budget balance and the price level?
options: Worsens the deficit and raises the price level
Improves the budget balance and lowers the price level
Improves the budget balance but raises the price level
Has no effect on the budget or price level
questions.explanation: Lower government spending reduces outlays, improving the budget balance (moves toward surplus). AD shifts left, reducing the price level—contractionary fiscal policy.

questions.question: Which action by the central bank regarding the 'bank rate' or 'discount rate' would be considered an expansionary monetary policy?
options: Keeping the bank rate constant indefinitely
questions.explanation: Lowering the bank rate makes borrowing from the central bank cheaper, encouraging bank lending and expanding the money supply—expansionary monetary policy.

questions.question: How does the central bank's sale of government bonds affect commercial bank reserves and the overnight interest rate?
options: Reserves increase and the overnight rate falls
Reserves decrease and the overnight rate rises
Reserves increase and the overnight rate rises
Reserves decrease and the overnight rate falls
questions.explanation: Selling bonds withdraws reserves from the banking system as banks pay for the bonds. Reduced reserves drive the overnight rate upward—contractionary monetary policy.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an automatic stabilizer that increases government expenditures during a recession without new legislation?
options: A new infrastructure bill passed by Congress
A one-time tax rebate enacted during recession
A legislated increase in defense spending
questions.explanation: Unemployment insurance automatically increases payouts when joblessness rises during a recession. This supports disposable income without requiring new legislation.

questions.question: According to the balanced budget multiplier, what is the net change in Real GDP if government spending and tax revenue both increase by \$40 billion?
questions.explanation: The balanced budget multiplier equals 1. When G and T increase equally, Real GDP increases by the amount of the spending increase: +\$40 billion.

questions.question: Which of the following occurs to the government's budget balance during an economic expansion as tax revenues rise and transfer payments fall?
options: The budget balance improves (moves toward surplus)
questions.explanation: During expansions, rising incomes boost tax revenue and falling unemployment reduces transfer payments (unemployment benefits). The budget balance automatically improves.

questions.question: In a floor system of monetary policy, which specific rate represents the interest the central bank pays to banks for holding excess reserves?
questions.explanation: In a floor system, the IOR (interest on reserves) sets the floor. Banks will not lend overnight below this rate since they can earn it risk-free from the central bank.

questions.question: Suppose the tax rate on capital income is 50 percent, the real interest rate is 3 percent, and the inflation rate is 4 percent. What is the real after-tax interest rate?
questions.explanation: Nominal interest rate = Real rate + Inflation = 3% + 4% = 7%. After-tax nominal return = 7% × (1 – 0.50) = 3.5%. Real after-tax return = After-tax nominal – Inflation = 3.5% – 4% = –0.5%. The return is negative because tax is paid on the inflation component of the nominal interest.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the impact of a lower overnight rate on the market value of existing fixed-rate bonds?
options: Bond prices are unaffected by overnight rates
Only stock prices are affected by rate changes
questions.explanation: Bond prices and interest rates move inversely. When rates fall, existing bonds with higher fixed coupons become more valuable, and their market prices rise.

questions.question: How does a decrease in the domestic overnight rate relative to foreign rates affect the international demand for the domestic currency?
options: Demand for the domestic currency increases
Demand for the domestic currency decreases
Demand is unaffected by interest rate differentials
Demand shifts only if the rate change is permanent
questions.explanation: Lower relative domestic rates reduce returns for foreign investors. They sell the domestic currency to invest elsewhere, decreasing demand and causing depreciation.

questions.question: Suppose the tax rate on capital income is 25 percent, the real interest rate is 4 percent, and the inflation rate is 4 percent. What is the real after-tax interest rate?
questions.explanation: Nominal interest rate = Real rate + Inflation = 4% + 4% = 8%. After-tax nominal return = 8% × (1 – 0.25) = 8% × 0.75 = 6%. Real after-tax return = After-tax nominal – Inflation = 6% – 4% = 2.0%.

questions.question: Raising the tax rate up along the Laffer curve creates which of the following outcomes?
options: Tax revenue rises then eventually falls
questions.explanation: The Laffer curve shows that tax revenue increases as the tax rate rises from low levels, reaches a maximum at some intermediate rate, and then declines if tax rates continue to rise beyond that peak—as higher rates increasingly discourage work and investment.

questions.question: The Country "TKland" has a budget deficit of $15 billion and a structural deficit of $3 billion. What is the cyclical deficit?
questions.explanation: Cyclical deficit = Total budget deficit – Structural deficit = $15B – $3B = $12 billion. The cyclical deficit reflects the portion of the deficit caused by the economy operating below potential (the business cycle), while the structural deficit would exist even at full employment.

questions.question: In a circular flow model including the government and foreign sectors, which of the following is categorized as a leakage?
questions.explanation: Leakages withdraw funds from the circular flow: Savings (S), Taxes (T), and Imports (M). Savings represent income not spent on domestic goods and services.

questions.question: Which policy mix would be most effective at increasing the share of GDP devoted to investment while keeping the price level stable?
options: Expansionary fiscal and expansionary monetary policy
Contractionary fiscal and expansionary monetary policy
Contractionary fiscal and contractionary monetary policy
Expansionary fiscal and contractionary monetary policy
questions.explanation: Contractionary fiscal policy restrains consumption and government spending, freeing resources for investment. Expansionary monetary policy lowers rates to encourage private investment. The mix supports capital formation while stabilizing prices.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the relationship between the unemployment rate and the inflation rate along the Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC)?
questions.explanation: The SRPC shows a short-run trade-off: lower unemployment is associated with higher inflation, and higher unemployment with lower inflation—an inverse relationship.

questions.question: What happens to the Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC) if the Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS) curve shifts to the right?
questions.explanation: A rightward LRAS shift (increased potential output) often lowers the natural rate or enables lower inflation at the same output. The SRPC shifts downward/leftward toward the origin — an improved trade-off.

questions.question: Which of the following identifies the point on the Phillips curve where the actual inflation rate is equal to the expected inflation rate?
options: The intersection of the SRPC and the LRPC
The lowest point on the SRPC
The vertical intercept of the LRPC
questions.explanation: At the natural rate of unemployment (NAIRU), actual inflation equals expected inflation. This is where the SRPC intersects the vertical LRPC—the long-run equilibrium.

questions.question: If the nominal wage increases by 4% and the real wage increases by 1%, what was the inflation rate for that period?
questions.explanation: Real wage change ≈ Nominal wage change – Inflation. 1% = 4% – Inflation → Inflation = 3%.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the effect on the real wage if the price level rises while the nominal wage remains 'sticky'?
options: The real wage becomes equal to the nominal wage
questions.explanation: Real wage = Nominal wage ÷ Price level. If prices rise and nominal wages are sticky, the real wage falls—workers can buy fewer goods with their fixed nominal wage.

questions.question: Which of the following identifies the state of the output gap when the actual unemployment rate is 4% and the natural rate of unemployment is 6%?
questions.explanation: Actual unemployment (4%) is below the natural rate (6%). This indicates Actual GDP exceeds Potential GDP—an inflationary (positive) output gap.

questions.question: What is the relationship between actual Real GDP and Potential GDP when the economy is at the 'peak' of the business cycle?
options: Actual GDP is well below Potential GDP
Actual GDP equals or exceeds Potential GDP
Potential GDP is declining while Actual GDP is rising
There is no relationship at the peak
questions.explanation: At the business cycle peak, economic activity is at its maximum. Actual GDP is typically at or above Potential GDP, often creating an inflationary gap.

questions.question: If the nominal exchange rate is 0.75 USD/CAD, calculate the real exchange rate if a basket of goods costs \$100 in Canada and \$80 in the USA.
questions.explanation: Price ratio = Canadian P ÷ US P = \$100 ÷ \$80 = 1.25. Real exchange rate = Nominal rate × Price ratio = 0.75 × 1.25 = 0.9375.

questions.question: As the economy enters a recession, which of the following events occur automatically?
options: Tax revenues rise and transfer payments fall
Tax revenues and transfer payments both rise
Government outlays rise and tax revenues fall
Government outlays and tax revenues both fall
questions.explanation: During a recession, automatic stabilizers kick in: tax revenues fall as incomes decline (progressive taxes collect less), while government outlays rise as more people claim unemployment benefits and other transfer payments. These automatic changes help cushion the downturn without new legislation.

questions.question: Which of the following would cause a rightward shift in the supply curve of a domestic currency in the foreign exchange market?
options: An increase in foreign demand for domestic exports
An increase in domestic demand for foreign imports
An increase in foreign investment inflows
A rise in domestic interest rates
questions.explanation: When domestic residents buy more imports, they must sell domestic currency to acquire foreign currency. This increases the supply of domestic currency in the forex market.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the impact of a domestic currency appreciation on the domestic price of imported goods?
questions.explanation: A stronger domestic currency buys more foreign currency. Imported goods cost less in domestic currency terms, reducing input costs and consumer prices for foreign products.
